stocks.note: Numbers as of market close April 2nd. Markets closed today (Good Friday).
stocks.news:
Markets ended the short week mixed, but the S&P 500 still notched its best week of 2026. Earnings season kicks off next week with Delta, Levi's, and Constellation Brands on deck.

Tesla (TSLA -5.42%) spun out after missing on car deliveries, as it has a surplus of around 50,000 cars. Deliveries came in at 358,023 — well below Wall Street expectations. The company has been pivoting toward robots and driverless cars, but hasn't made money from either. It's like pitching investors on a flying car while the garage is full of unsold sedans.

In other news, investors tried to pull 22% of Blue Owl's $36 billion credit fund — but the firm only let 5% out the door, leaving $3.2 billion in redemptions sitting unfilled. The fear? AI competition could make their software company loans riskier.

economy.commentary:
Oil stole the spotlight. U.S. crude surged 12% intraday to above $112 as the Strait of Hormuz situation remains unresolved. Gas prices hit $4.08 a gallon nationally — up $1.10 since the war started — and diesel cracked $5.51. Bay Area drivers are still paying well north of $6.

Gold pulled back to $4,676, giving up some of Tuesday's gains, after Trump's speech outlined a timeline investors found less reassuring than hoped.

On the trade front, yesterday marked the one-year anniversary of Liberation Day tariffs. The U.S. trade gap hit $57 billion in February — up 4.9% from January. AI hardware drove a big chunk of the import spike as companies kept stockpiling servers and chips. The tariffs were supposed to shrink the gap. So far? The opposite.

crypto.commentary:
Crypto kept sliding — BTC dropped another 1.56% to $67,014, ETH fell 3.18%, and XRP dipped to $1.32.

Hackers tied to North Korea stole $280 million from crypto exchange Drift earlier this week — adding to the $1.5 billion they took from Bybit last year. In a separate incident, suspected North Korean hackers slipped malware into Axios, a tool used by thousands of U.S. companies. Crypto security feels less like a feature and more like a wish list.

On the corporate side, Bitcoin treasury companies started dumping holdings to pay debt. Empery Digital, Genius Group, and Riot Platforms all unloaded billions in crypto. When prices fall and the bills come due, even the believers start selling.

real_estate.homebuilder: The 30-year fixed has climbed five straight weeks to its highest level since September — and the gap between sellers and buyers just hit a national record.
real_estate.commentary:
The seller-buyer gap just hit a record — 630,000 more people looking to sell than looking to buy. Homes are taking 53 days to sell (up from 48 last year), and the average monthly mortgage payment in the U.S. is now $2,742. Rising rates and global instability are keeping buyers on the sidelines.

Total inventory across our 16-city tracker slipped another 25 units to 2,522 — the fourth straight week of decline. Coming-soon held steady at 250, but new listings dropped sharply to 156 (from 207 last week).

📍 Milpitas: The hottest market got even hotter. Total inventory dropped 10 to 113 — the steepest decline of any city this week. Townhome pending (33) exceeds active (23) by 10. Coming-soon steady at 33. Buyers are snapping up homes before they even hit MLS.

📍 Fremont: Down 8 to 294 total. Coming-soon ticked up to 59 (from 50 last week). Townhome actives dropped from 50 to 47, and only 19 new listings. The spring pipeline is building but not releasing.

📍 Union City: Up 5 from last week to 89. Still one of the tightest markets, but inventory is slowly building with 6 new listings.

📍 Newark: Crossed the 100 mark — up 9 from last week to 101. Steady growth continues.

📍 Oakland: Biggest raw decline — down 20 to 736. Buyers absorbing faster than sellers can list.

📍 Dublin: Eased back slightly from 151 to 148, after last week's big jump.

On the national front, the record seller-buyer gap is a signal worth watching. More people want out than want in — and if mortgage rates stay elevated through spring, that gap could widen further.
